coredata.dc:title: Visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of ofloxacin by BiOBr nanocomposite modified with oxygen vacancies and N-doped CQDs: Enhanced photodegradation performance and mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zijing
Wang, Yang
Gao, Peng
Feng, Li
Zhang, Liqiu
Liu, Yongze
Du, Ziwen
coredata.dc:description: The rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers and weak light absorption capacity are two major challenges for bismuth-based photocatalysts. Here, N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr micro-flower photocatalysts with the visible-light activity were fabricated through the ethylene glycol solvothermal method for the first time, and oxygen vacancies (OVs) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were simultaneously introduced on the surface of BiOBr. OVs were introduced to form defective BiOBr (BiO1-xBr). N-CQDs and BiO1-xBr formed a strong binding effect. Then, the composition, morphology, crystal structure and photoelectric property of photocatalysts were studied, and the mechanism and pathway of ofloxacin (OFL) photodegradation were studied. N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr-4 was a micro-flower composed of nanosheets with a thickness of about 60 nm, this structure produced multiple light reflections. Photoelectrochemical analysis confirmed that the synergistic effect of OVs and N-CQDs significantly promoted the electron-hole separation (3 times vs BiOBr) and enhanced the light absorption range (Eg = 2.96 eV vs 3.24 eV). Meanwhile, the removal rate of OFL by N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr-4 was 6 times higher than that by BiOBr (Kobs of N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr-4 was 32 times higher than that of BiOBr). Electron spin resonances analysis and radical quenching experiments showed that ·O2 − and h+ played dominant roles in the OFL photodegradation system, and their contribution rates were 89.84% and 70.31%, respectively. There were main degradation pathways for OFL, including oxidation, dealkylation, hydroxylation and decarboxylation. This study explored the synergistic and complementary effects between OVs and N-CQDs, and provided a promising strategy for the photodegradation of toxic antibiotics by visible-light-driven photocatalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers and weak light absorption capacity are two major challenges for bismuth-based photocatalysts. Here, N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr micro-flower photocatalysts with the visible-light activity were fabricated through the ethylene glycol solvothermal method for the first time, and oxygen vacancies (OVs) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were simultaneously introduced on the surface of BiOBr. OVs were introduced to form defective BiOBr (BiO1-xBr). N-CQDs and BiO1-xBr formed a strong binding effect. Then, the composition, morphology, crystal structure and photoelectric property of photocatalysts were studied, and the mechanism and pathway of ofloxacin (OFL) photodegradation were studied. N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr-4 was a micro-flower composed of nanosheets with a thickness of about 60 nm, this structure produced multiple light reflections. Photoelectrochemical analysis confirmed that the synergistic effect of OVs and N-CQDs significantly promoted the electron-hole separation (3 times vs BiOBr) and enhanced the light absorption range (Eg = 2.96 eV vs 3.24 eV). Meanwhile, the removal rate of OFL by N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr-4 was 6 times higher than that by BiOBr (Kobs of N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr-4 was 32 times higher than that of BiOBr). Electron spin resonances analysis and radical quenching experiments showed that ·O2 − and h+ played dominant roles in the OFL photodegradation system, and their contribution rates were 89.84% and 70.31%, respectively. There were main degradation pathways for OFL, including oxidation, dealkylation, hydroxylation and decarboxylation. This study explored the synergistic and complementary effects between OVs and N-CQDs, and provided a promising strategy for the photodegradation of toxic antibiotics by visible-light-driven photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, quinolone antibiotics were widely used in human health care and animal disease treatment because of their broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, strong antibacterial ability and low price (Feng et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021). As a typical quinolone antibiotic, ofloxacin (OFL) is difficult to be removed effectively in sewage treatment plants. Therefore, OFL is often detected in surface water, groundwater and even drinking water (Su et al., 2022). It was reported that OFL had functional genes which could make bacteria resistant to drugs by induction and selection and was enriched in the human body through the food chain (Gullberg et al., 2011; Xue et al., 2015). Although its content was at the trace level, it would cause potential threats to creatures and even the ecological environment (Zhang et al., 2020). Therefore, developing effective methods for OFL removal has attracted much attention in the scientific community. Expectantly, photocatalytic technology becomes the research hotspot because of its high purification efficiency, fast reaction rate and strong oxidation ability (Cao et al., 2021; Feng et al., 2022; Zheng et al., 2022). However, many photocatalysts cannot effectively utilize visible light and their electron-hole pairs recombine rapidly, which limits their practical application (Lin et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2018a). Therefore, it makes sense to develop novel and efficient photocatalysts.

In recent years, BiOBr as an environment-friendly, low-cost, stably optical and chemical semiconductor photocatalyst, has attracted much attention (Hao et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2018). BiOBr has an open layered structure, [Bi2O2]2+ slabs are interleaved with double halogen anionic slabs, and an internal static electric field between them is favorable for the photocatalytic generated carrier separation (Ye et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2019). However, as a semiconductor photocatalyst, BiOBr inevitably has some disadvantages, such as narrow light absorption range and photocatalytic generated carrier recombination (Du et al., 2021; Tian et al., 2021). Consequently, it is urgent to develop a simple and easy solution to realize the enhanced photocatalytic performance of BiOBr materials.

Oxygen vacancies (OVs) on semiconductor photocatalysts are booming, and OVs have been proved to significantly improve the optical and electronic properties (Sedaghati et al., 2021). Firstly, OVs can fabricate defect states between the CB and VB (Pan et al., 2013). As an excellent electron trap, OVs can effectively capture photogenerated electrons and inhibit carrier recombination (Chen and Qiu, 2021; Zhao et al., 2019). Secondly, OVs can expand the light absorption range, because OVs can produce shallow donor states below the CB, thereby increasing sunlight collection and tuning the optical properties of photocatalysts (Miao et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2018b).

N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are effectively used to broaden the light absorption range, prolong the carrier life, and even improve the photocatalytic performance (Kim and Jo, 2020; Luo et al., 2021). The previous studies showed that the up-conversion photoluminescence properties of N-CQDs scattered ultraviolet light or light with the shorter wavelength and higher energy after absorbing visible light, and then improved the utilization of sunlight (Abd Rani et al., 2020; Rani et al., 2021). Meanwhile, as electron acceptors, N-CQDs can transfer electrons and reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers (Chen et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2021b).

Therefore, whether the synergistic effect of OVs and N-CQDs can obtain more significant photocatalytic performance and improve the degradation efficiency of OFL, and how to synergistically promote photoelectric properties and free radical production are worth studying.

Herein, synchronous load of OVs and N-CQDs on BiOBr surfaces was firstly prepared via ethylene glycol solvothermal method. As the target antibiotic, OFL was used to demonstrate photocatalytic degradation performance of N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr. The purposes of this study included the following: (1) to study the composition, microstructure, photoelectric properties of N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr; (2) to investigate the photocatalytic efficiency of N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr for OFL under different pH values, coexisting ions and dissolved organic matter conditions; (3) to explore the degradation mechanism of OFL by N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr according to X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), photoelectric property detection, active species quenching experiment and electron spin resonances (ESR) analysis; (4) to determine the possible photodegradation pathways of OFL.
In this study, the used materials were shown in Text. S1.

N-CQDs were fabricated through the hydrothermal method (Ji et al., 2018). Generally, 10 mmol of ammonium citrate was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, and then 710 μL of ethylenediamine was added into the above solution. The solution was stirred for 30 min and poured into 50 mL of stainless steel reactor and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. Finally, the sample was cooled down to the room temperature naturally. The solution was filtered through the 0.22 μm membrane filter to get pure N-CQDs solution. Meanwhile, N-CQDs solid was obtained by cold drying machine, and the solid content of N-CQDs solution was 90 mg/mL.

BiO1-xBr photocatalyst was synthesized by solvothermal method. Firstly, 4 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added into 40 mL of ethylene glycol and dispersed by ultrasound until it was completely dissolved into the transparent solution. Secondly, 4 mmol of KBr was added into 40 mL of ethylene glycol and stirred, this solution was dropped into the above solution and stirred at room temperature for 1 h. The mixture was poured into 100 mL of stainless steel reactor, heated at 180 °C for 6 h, and then cooled down to the room temperature naturally. The sample was centrifuged at 8000 rpm, washed by ultrapure water and ethanol several times respectively, and then dried at 60 °C. The original BiOBr was synthesized by the same procedure, but the difference was that ethylene glycol was replaced by ultrapure water.

N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr photocatalyst was fabricated through solvothermal method. Firstly, 4 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added into 40 mL of ethylene glycol and dispersed by ultrasound until it was completely dissolved. N-CQDs were added into above solution and magnetically stirred for 20 min. Secondly, 4 mmol of KBr was added into 40 mL of ethylene glycol and stirred until it was completely dissolved. Finally, steps and synthetic conditions of Bi1-xBr preparation were adopted. The as-prepared samples were defined as CBB-Y (N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr is expressed as CBB, and Y is the volume of N-CQDs solution), such as CBB-3, CBB-4, CBB-5, CBB-6, and CBB-7. For comparison, N-CQDs/BiOBr-4mL (expressed as COBB-4) was synthesized in ultrapure water system by the same method.
Characterizations were shown in Text. S2. Photoelectrochemical measurements were shown in Text. S3.

Photocatalytic degradation experiments were performed in a photocatalytic reactor. The visible light as light source was composed of a xenon lamp (300 W) and a 400 nm cutoff filter. Specifically, initial OFL concentration was 10 mg/L 50 mg photocatalysts were dispersed into photoreactor (100 mL). First, the photocatalysts were stirred in the OFL solution for 60 min under dark condition until the adsorption-desorption equilibrium was achieved. Next, approximately 4.0 mL solution was taken out from the reactor with a syringe every 30 min. The photocatalysts from each solution were removed by the 0.22 μm microporous filter. Ultraviolet spectrophotometer (PE lambda 750) at 283 nm was performed to detect the concentration of OFL. The mineralization degree of OFL was evaluated by measuring the variations of total organic carbon (TOC) concentration through a Shimadzu TOCVPH instrument. Radical capture experiments were launched by introducing free radical scavengers which interacted with free radicals in the photocatalytic process. ·O2 − was captured by p-benzoquinone (PBQ, 10.0 mM), h+ was captured by sodium oxalate (SA, 10.0 mM), and ·OH was captured by tert-Butyl alcohol (TBA, 10.0 mM) and isopropanol (IPA, 10.0 mM). All the above experiments were carried out parallel experiments.

The crystal structures of photocatalysts were shown in XRD results (Fig. 1 ). In Fig. 1a, the observed diffraction peak was extensive due to the small size of the N-CQDs. The wide peak at about 2θ = 26° was indexed to the graphitic carbon (JCPDS file No. 008–0415) (Sun et al., 2014). The relatively sharp peak around 11° indicated the presence of micro-crystallite, the peak at 19.5° was attributed to the diffraction of graphite (002) crystalline planes (Aghamali et al., 2018), and the weak broad peak at 40° indicated the partial graphitization of N-CQDs (Liang et al., 2013). Fig. 1b showed the XRD patterns of other synthetic photocatalysts, and the distinct diffraction peaks of BiOBr with 2θ at 10.90°, 21.90°, 25.21°, 31.72°, 32.27°, 34.16°, 44.66°, 50.65°, and 57.20° were observed, which could be indexed to (001), (002), (101), (102), (110), (112), (004), (104), and (212) crystal planes of BiOBr (PDF#85–0862). The sharp diffraction peaks revealed a high degree of crystallization (Yu et al., 2020). For BiO1-xBr, the position of diffraction peaks did not change significantly compared with BiOBr. However, the intensity of some peaks, such as (001), (002), (101), (102), (112), (104), and (212), decreased slightly, indicating lower crystallinity of these exposed surfaces in the ethylene glycol. Theoretically, the electrons of ethylene glycol can reduce Bi, which destroys the Bi–O bonds and leads to the separation of oxygen atoms from BiOBr, thus leading to the formation of OVs in BiOBr photocatalyst. For COBB-4 and N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr composite photocatalysts with different N-CQDs doping dosage, the N-CQDs characteristic peaks could not be shown due to the small crystallinity, low dosage and high dispersion. Additionally, no other impure crystal phases were detected. These results showed that the composite photocatalysts were successfully prepared without any other impurities. The influence of different hydrothermal temperatures and times on the crystal structure of CBB-4 was also explored, and the results were shown in Fig. S1. In order to explore the surface functional groups of photocatalysts, FT-IR and Raman were carried out, and the results and analysis were shown in the Fig. S2.

SEM showed the morphologies of the materials. In Fig. 2 a–b, BiOBr presented a typical sheet structure with a highly smooth surface. It could be measured that the diameter of BiOBr sheets was about 1.5–2.5 μm and the thickness was about 80 nm. CBB-4 presented a regular micro-flower with the diameter of about 1.75 μm in Fig. 2c–d. Additionally, each micro-flower was composed of many nanosheets with a thickness of about 60 nm. The presence of OVs changed the surface morphology of BiOBr. It was reported that this hierarchical cross-layer structure could improve the light capture ability by producing multiple light reflections (Dong et al., 2011; Shao et al., 2011). The decrease of surface potential caused by ethylene glycol led to a coarse surface, which made the catalysts have a higher specific surface area, shorter diffusion path and more reaction sites (Yu et al., 2020). The microstructure was further observed by HRTEM. In Fig. 2e, CBB-4 showed microspheres with a diameter of 1.75 μm, which was consistent with SEM results. The N-CQDs showed a quasi-spherical morphology with an average diameter of 8 nm (Fig. 2h). Uniformly distributed N-CQDs were observed (red circles) on the surface of BiO1-xBr micro-flower, and the obvious local lattice deletion/dislocation (yellow arrows) possibly caused by surface O atoms escape could be observed (Yu et al., 2020). The HRTEM image (Fig. 2f) clearly showed the crystal structure of CBB-4, the lattice fringe with the spacing of 0.277 nm was coincided with crystal plane (110) of BiOBr (Di et al., 2016). In insert of Fig. 2g, the selected area electron diffraction showed bright diffraction spots and obvious diffraction ring distribution, indicating that CBB-4 had polycrystalline structure and great crystallinity (Gao et al., 2021). Meanwhile, N-CQDs were loaded on BiO1-xBr nanosheets. The lattice fringe with spacing of 0.331 nm was coincided with crystal plane (002) of graphite phase. In Fig. 2i-n, EDX-mapping analysis showed that Br, O, Bi, C and N elements were detected and evenly dispersed, proving that N-CQDs were successfully loaded on BiOBr.

To verify the existence of OVs in BiO1-xBr and CBB-4 photocatalysts, ESR analysis was performed (Fig. S3). Pure BiOBr and COBB-4 showed no significant signal. However, BiO1-xBr and CBB-4 showed significant signal at g = 2.002, which was a typical signal of OVs and firmly proved the successful introduction of OVs on the surface of BiOBr (Guo et al., 2021). Additionally, the OVs could induce indirect subband excitation, thus changing the optical properties of BiOBr. As expected, unlike white BiOBr, the color of BiO1-xBr was light yellow, which was reflected by the continuous and exponentially attenuated absorption tail of the whole visible region and had a slight band edge offset (Li et al., 2015).

In Fig. 3 , the surface electronic state and element valence state were determined by XPS spectra. In Fig. 3a, Bi, O and Br elements were contained in BiOBr, BiO1-xBr and CBB-4. In Fig. 3b, peaks of 159.1 and 164.4 eV owning to characteristic peaks of Bi3+ in BiOBr, were corresponded to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively (Zhang et al., 2014). The Bi 4f peak in CBB-4 slightly shifted 0.3 eV towards the lower binding energy compared with BiOBr, indicating that OVs were formed in the [Bi2O2]2+ layer of BiO1-xBr due to charge compensation (Zhao et al., 2015). Specifically, the absence of some O atoms led to the reduction of the coordination number of Bi3+ (Yu et al., 2018), the free electrons occupied the position of O2− in regular lattices and made the surrounding electrons localized. In Fig. 3c, two peaks could be clearly identified from the O1s spectrum. The peaks at 529.7 and 531.0 eV belonged to lattice oxygen in the [Bi2O2]2+ layer and oxygen atoms near OVs, respectively (Tong et al., 2019). In pure BiOBr, the relative contents of lattice oxygen and oxygen atoms near OVs were 81.8% and 18.2%, respectively. In CBB-4, the relative intensity of the peak at 529.7 eV decreased (the relative content decreased to 52.6%), while that of 531.0 eV increased (the relative content increased to 47.4%) because of the chemisorption of oxygen species. Combined with the ESR results that there was no OVs in BiOBr, it was proven that OVs were successfully introduced into CBB-4 photocatalyst. (An et al., 2022). Peaks of 68.0 and 68.9 eV (Fig. 3d) owning to characteristic peaks of Br−, were corresponded to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2, respectively (Dai et al., 2021). The Br 3 d peak of CBB-4 slightly shifted 0.3 eV towards the higher binding energy, indicating N-CQDs interacted with BiO1-xBr and N-CQDs had the ability to capture electrons. From the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3e), C 1s spectrum of BiOBr could be divided into the peaks of 284.6 (C=C/C–C bond) and 286.0 eV (C–O bond). In the hydrothermal reaction system of ethylene glycol and water, these reaction media containing hydroxyl groups would induce the formation of hydroxyl and other oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the materials during the reaction process (Gao et al., 2022). It has been reported that the photocatalytic performance of BiOI nanosheets could be improved by regulating hydroxyl group (Wang et al., 2020). Similarly, C1s spectrum of CBB-4 could be divided into three peaks (284.6, 286.0 and 288.4 eV). That of 288.4 eV (C–N bond) indicated that N-CQDs were hybridized into BiOBr. The N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3f) of CBB-4 could be divided into the peaks of 399.4 and 400.9 eV belonging to C–N–C and N–(C)3 bonds, respectively (Zhang et al., 2018). Consequently, N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr had many chemical polar functional groups (e.g. amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups), and N-CQDs were successfully loaded on BiO1-xBr by chemisorption (Gao et al., 2020). These results were consistent with HRTEM, EDX-mapping, FT-IR and Raman analysis.

The photodegradation performance of OFL by as-prepared photocatalysts was shown in Fig. 4 a. The optimal dosage of CBB-4 was 0.5 g/L (Fig. S4). It was found that direct OFL photodegradation was not obvious when the visible light was irradiated for 150 min, indicating that OFL was very durable. The removal rate of OFL by BiOBr was 13.2%. After OVs and N-CQDs were formed on BiOBr, the removal rate of OFL increased to 23.5% and 31.2%, respectively. For N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr, the OFL removal rates of CBB-3, CBB-4, CBB-5, CBB-6 and CBB-7 were 51.0%, 78.5%, 72.0%, 68.2% and 63.0%, respectively. The results showed that more OFL was degraded as the amount of N-CQDs increased. The optimal N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr photocatalyst was CBB-4, and the removal rate of OFL was almost 6 times that of the BiOBr (13.2%). However, excessive N-CQDs would adversely affect the photocatalytic efficiency, such as CBB-5 (72.0%), CBB-6 (68.2%), CBB-7 (63.0%) < CBB-4 (78.5%). The calculated Kobs was shown in Fig. 4b. Kobs presented the following order: CBB-4 (0.0128 min−1) > CBB-5 (0.0099 min−1) > CBB-3 (0.0061 min−1) > CBB-6 (0.0053 min−1) > CBB-7 (0.0043 min−1) > COBB-4 (0.0026 min−1) > BiO1-xBr (0.0018 min−1) > BiOBr (0.0004 min−1). Clearly, it was shown that Kobs of CBB-4 was 0.0128 min−1, 32 times that of the BiOBr (0.0004 min−1). The removal rate of OFL presented the following order: N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr > N-CQDs/BiOBr > BiO1-xBr, indicating that synergistic effect of OVs and N-CQDs was much greater than that of single effect. In Table S1, CBB-4 photocatalyst had good photodegradation performance of OFL compared with the reported photocatalysts. Therefore, CBB-4 was selected for further research because of its optimal photocatalytic performance in this system. In Fig. 4c, the TOC removal rate by CBB-4 was about 60.1% within 1.5 h, which indicated that the mineralization ability of CBB-4 was remarkable. This study explored the degradation efficiency of other antibiotics (such as quinolones, sulfamethoxazole, carbamazepine and tetracycline) by CBB-4 photocatalyst. The results were shown in Fig. S5. It could be seen that CBB-4 had good removal efficiency on them. In recent years, there have been many studies on CQDs modified Bi-based photocatalysts. This study compared the properties and photodegradation efficiency of these materials, and the results were shown in Table S2 and Fig. S6.

The cycling experiment results of photocatalysts were shown in Fig. S7, the removal rate of OFL slightly decreased from 78.5% to 73.5% after three cycles. Besides, the XRD, FT-IR and XPS patterns of the spent CBB-4 after cycle experiments were shown in Fig. S8. It could be seen that the materials had no obvious distinction after three cycles, implying the crystalline structure and phase of CBB-4 did not change after the photocatalysis reaction. The main elements in CBB-4 could be found through element mapping measurement (Fig. S9). Meanwhile, the ESR spectra (Fig. S10) showed that the OVs contained in CBB-4 still maintained similar strength to the fresh CBB-4 after cycle experiments. Therefore, the prepared CBB-4 had excellent photocatalytic performance, high stability and reusability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of influence factors on photocatalytic performance of OFL
ce:section.ce:para:
The effects of pH values on OFL photodegradation performance by CBB-4 were shown in Figs. S11a–b. With the increase of pH (2.0 → 5.0 → 11.0), the removal rate (55.2% → 74.5% → 49.7%) and Kobs (0.0096 → 0.0151 → 0.0046 min-1) of OFL increased and then decreased. Obviously, the pH value strongly affected the removal rate of OFL. The degradation rate under weak acidic (pH = 5) was much faster than that under strong acidic (2, 3), neutral (7) and alkaline (10). This phenomenon could be explained from the free radical action. With the decrease of pH, ·O2 − would combine with H+ to produce H2O2, and ·O2 − that might play direct oxidation roles would be reduced (eqs. (1) and (2)) (Dong et al., 2018). With the increase of pH, OH− would consume part of h+, reduce the content of h+, and then inhibit the degradation of OFL (eq. (3)) (Mamba and Mishra, 2016). Besides, due to protonation or deprotonation, different pH values would affect the surface charge of photocatalysts, as shown in Fig. S12. Meanwhile, the effects of coexisting ions (Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−) and DOM were shown in Fig. S11.
(1) ·O2 − + H+ → HOO·
(2) 2HOO· → H2O2 + O2
(3) h+ + 4OH− → O2 + 2H2O

In Fig. 5 a–b, the light absorption ability and electronic band structure of photocatalysts were studied by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra. BiOBr mainly absorbed ultraviolet light and hardly absorbed visible and infrared light. After introducing OVs, the light absorption of BiO1-xBr had a red-shift, which could be attributed to the energy level springboard effect of OVs. Specifically, OVs makes free electrons occupy the position of O2−, to minimize the energy of vacancy formation, and form shallow donor energy levels below the CB (Wang et al., 2018b). After introducing N-CQDs, the absorption performance of COBB-4 to visible light was enhanced. The absorption edge of BiO1-xBr modified by N-CQDs was significantly widened, especially in the wavelength range of 425–800 nm, mainly due to up-conversion photoluminescence performance and the strong light absorption capacity of N-CQDs (Li et al., 2018). In Fig. 5b, plots of (F (R∞)hν)1/2 -(hν) versus photo energy could calculate the band gap (Eg) of photocatalysts (Yin et al., 2020). The band gaps of BiOBr, BiO1-xBr, CBB-4 and COBB-4 were calculated to be 3.24, 3.11, 2.96, and 3.16 eV, respectively. The light absorption capacity of photocatalysts became stronger with the decrease of the band gap energy.

The separation and transport efficiencies of photocarriers were reflected by PL spectra and TRPL spectra. The key factor to improve photocatalytic performance is to prolong the lifetime of photocarriers in photodegradation reactions (Ji et al., 2018). Fig. 5c showed PL spectra of BiOBr, BiO1-xBr, CBB-4 and COBB-4 at 320 nm. Obviously, BiOBr showed the highest PL intensity, indicating strong charge recombination. However, OVs and N-CQDs weakened the PL intensity of BiOBr, indicating that they inhibited the carrier recombination. Specifically, OVs can form a trapping level that captures photogenerated electrons and quenches the photoluminescence signal (Zhang et al., 2011). N-CQDs had conjugated π structure, which could accelerate electron transfer and effectively separate photogenerated carriers (Tian et al., 2015). However, CBB-4 showed the lowest PL intensity when OVs and N-CQDs were simultaneously introduced into BiOBr. In Fig. S14, the prolonged radiation lifetime fully confirmed that OVs and N-CQDs inhibited the recombination of photogenerated carriers of BiOBr and improved migration efficiency. Meanwhile, the emission lifetime of BiO1-xBr was longer than that of BiOBr, indicating that the relaxation processes of residential charge carriers were significantly different. It has been reported that the excitonic effect mediated by Coulomb interactions between photogenerated electrons and holes played an important role in the photoinduced process. The conversion of excitons to free carriers was beneficial to the optimization of carriers involved in photocatalysis. Energy disorder had positive effects on exciton dissociation, and active carriers were expected to appear in photocatalysts with energy disorder. Theoretically, there was a strong localized charge density near OVs, leading to energy disorder. Fig. S14 demonstrated that exciton dissociation could be carried out in the presence of OVs. On the basis of exciton dissociation, we further explored whether the introduction of OVs could enhance charge carriers. In this study, molecular oxygen activation was measured under visible light, and the results were shown in Fig. S15. It was clear that both samples produced reactive oxygen species. Obviously, the BiO1-xBr had the ability to promote the activation of molecular oxygen. Specific reactive oxygen species could be seen from the following experiments.

Additionally, the up-conversion luminescence effect of N-CQDs was measured by up-conversion PL spectroscopy, and the results were shown in Fig. 5d. At excitation wavelengths ranging from 550 to 900 nm, the corresponding up-conversion emission wavelengths were located at 311 nm (550 nm), 312 nm (600 nm), 330 nm (650 nm), 355 nm (700 nm), 380 nm (750 nm), 403 nm (800 nm), 430 nm (850 nm) and 453 nm (900 nm), respectively. The strongest peak was 330 nm (650 nm), and the second-strongest peak was 355 nm (700 nm), indicating that the unique up-conversion characteristic of N-CQDs absorbed light with long wavelength and scattered light with short wavelength. Therefore, N-CQDs could endow carrier photocatalysts with visible light response ability. In order to compare the up-conversion characteristics of N-CQDs in this work and previous work, Table S3 listed the Bi-based photocatalysts modified by N-CQDs in recent years, and analyzed the up-conversion characteristics and modification properties of N-CQDs. According to the Table S3, N-CQDs could absorb long wavelength light and scatter short wavelength light. However, N-CQDs prepared by different preparation methods had different up-conversion characteristics, such as different emitting wavelength range and emitting intensity. In this study, the strongest emitting wavelength of N-CQDs was 330 nm, and the second-strongest emitting wavelength was 355 nm. The scattering light energy of N-CQDs was higher than that of other N-CQDs, so N-CQDs had excellent up-conversion characteristics. Meanwhile, the degradation performance of N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr photocatalyst after the introduction of N-CQDs was higher than that of BiOBr photocatalyst in the degradation of pollutants. Therefore, N-CQDs were the promising material for modifying photocatalysts.

The photoelectron transformation ability of photocatalysts was analyzed by I-t and EIS. Generally, the higher photocurrent intensity indicates the faster separation speed of photogenerated carriers (Chen et al., 2018). In Fig. 5e, CBB-4 (1.75 μA/cm2) had the highest photocurrent density, suggesting the highest separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. BiO1-xBr (0.8 μA/cm2) and COBB-4 (1.1 μA/cm2) showed higher photocurrent density than BiOBr (0.6 μA/cm2). COBB-4 had higher photocurrent density than BiO1-xBr. It could be inferred that the capturing electron characteristics of N-CQDs could improve electron-hole separation more effectively than OVs. Further introducing OVs, the photoelectrons of CB were transferred to the coordination unsaturated sites of OVs, resulting in the improvement of carrier separation efficiency. The results of EIS were shown in Fig. 5f. Materials with smaller arc radius have smaller charge transfer resistance and stronger photogenerated carrier separation ability (Jia et al., 2020). The radius of the sample followed the order of BiOBr > BiO1-xBr > COBB-4 > CBB-4. Obviously, CBB-4 presented the smallest arc radius, which meant that CBB-4 photocatalyst had the highest carrier separation efficiency. Meanwhile, the carrier separation efficiency of COBB-4 was higher than that of BiO1-xBr. These results were consistent with I-t. I-t and EIS results showed that the simultaneous introduction of OVs and N-CQDs to BiOBr could improve photogenerated carrier separation ability. Compared with OVs, the introduction of N-CQDs played a major role in facilitating the separation of photocarriers and prolonging the lifetime of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

The dominant active species formed in the photodegradation of OFL by CBB-4 photocatalyst were studied in the radical quenching experiments (Fig. 6 a). PBQ, SA, IPA and TBA with different concentrations acted as quenchers for ·O2 −, h+ and ·OH, respectively. When no quencher was added, the removal rate of OFL was 78.5%. After adding 10.0 mM quenchers, the removal rates of OFL decreased to 21.0%, 45.0%, 78.4% and 78.0%, respectively. Additionally, the photocatalytic inhibition rate (R) under different radical scavengers could be obtained (eqs. (4)–(6)).

The results were shown in Table S4. When PBQ and SA were added, the photocatalytic inhibition rates were 89.84% and 70.31%, respectively, indicating ·O2 − acted as the predominant active species in N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr photodegradation system, and h+ also had the relative high contribution. After adding IPA or TBA, the inhibition rates were 3.13% and 6.25%, respectively, suggesting ·OH had the minimal contribution. Therefore, the quenching experiments preliminarily confirmed that ·O2 − and h+ acted as dominant active species during N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr photodegradation system.

ESR experiments could further verify the existence of reactive oxygen species in the reaction systems. According to Fig. 6b–c, there was no ESR peak signal under dark condition. CBB-4 photocatalyst showed characteristic signals of 1:1:1:1 DMPO-·O2 − quadruple peaks and 1:2:2:1 DMPO-·OH quadruple peaks after visible light irradiation, indicating that CBB-4 generated/transformed ·O2 − and ·OH. Meanwhile, the ESR signal intensities increased with the increase of visible light irradiation time, suggesting that there were more reactive oxygen species produced. Furthermore, in order to investigate possible photodegradation mechanisms of improving degradation efficiency and complex oxidation processes, the band structure of CBB-4 was proposed. In Fig. 6d, the VB positions of BiOBr and CBB-4 composite were determined by XPS valence spectra. The highest occupied electronic density of state for CBB-4 and BiOBr were determined to be 1.98 eV. They had the similar valence-band maximum (VBM) value, suggesting that the electronic structure of BiOBr was not affected after the introduction of OVs and N-CQDs. The corrected value of XPS valence spectra was about 0.63 eV (referring to NHE at pH = 7), VBM values of BiOBr and CBB-4 were calculated to be 2.61 eV (Liu et al., 2009). In Fig. 5b, it could be determined that the band gaps (Eg) of BiOBr and CBB-4 were 3.24 and 2.96 eV by using the Tauc plot, respectively. According to the formula (ECB = EVB – Eg), the conduction-band minimum (CBM) of BiOBr and CBB-4 were calculated to be −0.63 and −0.35 eV, respectively.

The photocatalytic degradation mechanism of N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr nanocomposite photocatalyst can be understood from the sketch shown in Fig. 7 . When suspended BiOBr in OFL solution absorbed photons of energy (hν) greater than/equal to Eg, free electrons of BiOBr were excited and transferred from VB to CB. These free electrons reacted with O2 adsorbed on the photocatalyst surface or dissolved in water and O2 were reduced to ·O2 − since the CB potential of CBB-4 (−0.63eV) was more negative than the O2/·O2 − potential (−0.046 eV vs NHE). h+ had strong oxidation ability and directly participated in photocatalytic degradation because the VB potential of BiOBr was positive (+2.61 eV). Additionally, the h+ could oxide H2O to ·OH since the VB potential of CBB-4 (+2.61eV) was more positive than the H2O/·OH potential (+1.99 eV vs NHE). However, radical capturing experiments showed that ·OH had little effect on OFL degradation. Therefore, as the dominant active species, ·O2 − and h+ oxidized OFL molecules to small molecular products such as CO2 and H2O. Generally, the photocatalytic activity of BiOBr was limited due to low light-utilization ability and low photocarrier separation efficiency under visible light irradiation. However, when OVs was introduced into BiOBr, OVs could produce shallow donor states below the CB, thereby increasing sunlight collection and tuning the optical properties of photocatalysts. Additionally, OVs provided abundant coordination unsaturated sites for O2 with the reliable chemisorption, and electrons were extracted from OVs to form ·O2 − through two-electron reduction. Meanwhile, unique up-conversion photoluminescence performance of N-CQDs absorbed light with long wavelength and scattered light with short wavelength to expand the light absorption range and produce more photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The synergism of hierarchical structure of BiO1-xBr with multiple light reflections and up-conversion photoluminescence of N-CQDs enhanced visible light absorption ability. N-CQDs could also enrich electrons, accelerate the effective transfer of excited electrons from BiOBr to N-CQDs surface, and effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated charges. More adsorbed oxygen on the surface could be reduced to ·O2 − due to the effective utilization of photogenerated electrons. Therefore, OVs and N-CQDs synergistically improved the photocatalytic properties of BiOBr materials.

The transformation/degradation products of OFL in the CBB-4 photodegradation system were identified by LC-MS/MS analysis. In Tables S5 and 27 transformation products were identified. According to Frontier electron density and Frontier Orbital Theory on atoms of OFL reported in the literature, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of OFL was mainly located on the piperazine ring, which was relatively easy to lose electrons for oxidation (Zhang et al., 2020). The N atoms (N1, N4) on the piperazine ring were the most active atoms, which were preferentially attacked by h+. C2, C3, C20, and C22 atoms were more easily attacked by ·O2 − (Abd El-Hamid et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019). Consequently, oxidation and cleavage of piperazine and oxazine ring were considered as the main photodegradation pathways of OFL, which were proved by LC-MS/MS analysis.

In Fig. S16, four degradation pathways of OFL were shown in the LC-MS/MS results. Pathway I was openness of the piperazine ring, and ·O2 − played an important role. Firstly, OFL was attacked by ·O2 − and the piperazine ring was opened to form TP391. As an aldehyde derivative, the instability of TP391 led to further oxidation with three reaction processes (Zhao et al., 2021a). The first reaction process occurred as dealkylation to form TP377c and subsequent extrusion of –CO group from TP377c to produce TP349a and TP349b. Finally, the cleavage of nitrogen-containing alkylation of TP349a formed TP306. In the second reaction process, the –CO group was extruded from TP391 to form TP363a and TP363b, respectively. Afterwards, decarboxylation and hydroxylation occurred successively to form TP319, TP379. In the third reaction process, hydroxylation, oxidation and decarboxylation reaction occurred successively to form TP407, TP405 and TP361. The cleavage of –C2H4– and subsequent –C2H4–NH–CH3 on the piperazine ring formed TP335 and TP278, respectively. TP335 could be converted into TP337 by hydrogenation process. Pathway Ⅱ proceeded as demethylation reaction of the piperazine ring, which was mainly initiated by h+. Demethylation reaction appeared at N4 and then produced TP347. TP347 was decarboxylated to form TP303. The cleavage of the piperazine ring and subsequent –C2H4–NH2 from TP347 formed TP321and TP278, respectively. Pathway Ⅲ was hydroxylation of N, O-containing heterocyclic ring and the piperazine ring, ·O2 − played an important role. The hydroxyl group addition formed TP377a and TP377b, and further formed TP375a and TP375b due to oxidation. Generally, the attack of ·O2 − on organic matter is usually carried out through hydrogen atom transfer mechanism (Hayyan et al., 2016). Because ·O2 − has the relatively high energy, it does not act as a direct one-electron oxidant. Radical-related structure transformed hydroxyl group after the ·O2 − attack (Warren et al., 2010). Pathway Ⅳ was decarboxylation reaction. ·O2 − directly attacked the carboxyl group to form TP317. A simpler organic compound of TP128 was obtained after a series of reactions. Most of identified intermediates underwent oxidation and might eventually be mineralized into CO2 and H2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prediction for the toxicity of OFL and its intermediates based on quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR)
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the toxicity of OFL and its intermediates, Toxicity Estimation Software (T.E.S.T.) was used to predict the toxicity based on QSAR prediction, and the results were shown in Fig. S17. The acute toxicity assessed by LC50 by fathead minnow was shown in Fig. S17a. OFL was defined as “very toxic” (LC50 = 0.23 mg/L). TP335, TP391 and TP347 showed higher LC50 than OFL after the initial attack, indicating that the acute toxicity was reduced. After further oxidation, most of the organic compounds were considered “toxic” (eg. TP291, TP321, TP361), “harmful” (TP306) and “not harmful” (TP128), and few of them were “very toxic”. Even the LC50 of TP275a and TP375b were lower than OFL, but their contents were small. The LC50 of TP377b was lower than OFL, but after further oxidation, TP321, TP278 and TP303 become “toxic” and “harmful”, respectively, and the toxicity was reduced. Generally, the toxicity decreased after OFL degradation. The bioaccumulation factor was shown in Fig. S17b. The bioaccumulation factor decreased after the initial attack (except TP377b and TP347). After further oxidation, Organic compounds except TP303 and TP337 (contents were low) showed lower bioaccumulation factors compared with OFL, indicating that the bioaccumulation effect was reduced after photodegradation. Mutagenicity was shown in Fig. S17c. Except TP292, organic compounds showed reduced mutagenicity and even negative mutagenicity (<0.5). Therefore, it is recommended to prolong the photocatalysis time to effectively remove OFL and minimize the intermediates that may be toxic to the aquatic environment.

Consequently, a series of novel visible-light active N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr photocatalysts for OFL degradation were successfully synthesized by ethylene glycol solvent method for the first time. N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr was a hybrid micro-flower composed of nanosheets with an average thickness of 60 nm, this structure improved light capture ability by producing multiple light reflections. The synergistic effect of OVs and N-CQDs significantly improved the effective surface charge transfer, broadened the light absorption range and then promoted the photodegradation performance. OVs had the shallow donor level and electron trap effect, N-CQDs had the up-conversion photoluminescence and electron bridge effect. Meanwhile, N-CQDs/BiO1-xBr-4 had the highest photocatalytic activity, the removal rate of OFL was 78.5% after visible light irradiation for 2.5 h ·O2 − and h+ played predominant roles in the OFL photocatalytic degradation system. According to LC-MS/MS analysis, the main degradation pathways of OFL included oxidation, dealkylation, hydroxylation and decarboxylation. After degradation, toxicity of OFL and its intermediates was reduced. This study could provide a novel idea for synergistic effect between OVs and quantum dots to improve photocatalytic activity, promote the development of broad-spectrum response BiOBr based photocatalysts for the degradation of refractory antibiotics.

Zijing Zhang: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis. Yang Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Peng Gao: Validation, Investigation. Li Feng and Liqiu Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yongze Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Ziwen Du: Validation, Investigation.
